title: From Big CNNs to Efficient CNNs
concept: CNN history gradually shifted from winning by brute-force depth and width toward winning by smarter building blocks, compute reuse, and hardware-aware efficiency — setting up EfficientNet's next idea of scaling models more systematically.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what is the key lesson of an accuracy-efficiency tradeoff?

options.text: The best model depends on the hardware budget and deployment goal, not only on benchmark score
Efficiency matters only before training, never at inference time
Every task should use the biggest model available

exercises.explain: Exactly — now let's place that lesson in the bigger story of CNN architecture history.

cards.title: The story changed from 'more' to 'smarter'
cards.body: Early famous CNNs often looked like a contest in scale: more layers, more channels, bigger stacks. That worked for a while. But over time, designers learned that architecture quality also comes from how information and compute are organized, especially when models must leave the data center and run in the real world.

cards.title: What changed in this era?
cards.body: This era of CNN design moved from brute-force scaling toward efficient structure. ResNet improved information flow for very deep networks, DenseNet emphasized feature reuse, SqueezeNet cut parameter count with bottlenecks, and MobileNet cut compute by redesigning the core convolutional block. The shared theme is smarter use of limited budget.

cards.title: Cities stop growing by only making taller buildings
cards.body: A city cannot solve every problem by building taller towers forever. Eventually it needs better roads, transit, zoning, and utility design. CNNs followed a similar path: not just taller and wider, but better internal infrastructure.

cards.title: Different architectures, same deeper lesson
cards.body: VGG showed the power of clean repeated blocks. Inception showed modular design and compute tradeoffs. ResNet fixed deep training with skips. DenseNet reused features directly. SqueezeNet and MobileNet made compact deployment a first-class goal. The field kept asking the same question in new ways: how can we spend computation more intelligently?

cards.title: Efficiency is not anti-accuracy
cards.body: The point of efficient CNNs is not 'accuracy no longer matters.' The point is that accuracy must be earned within a budget. Good architecture finds better tradeoffs, so the network wastes less of its parameters and compute.

cards.title: Brute-force scaling vs. efficient design
cards.body: Both aim for strong vision performance, but they push in different ways.

left.text:
Add depth or width
Spend more parameters + compute
Hope scale alone wins

right.text:
Redesign blocks carefully
Reuse features and cut waste
Optimize for real hardware too

exercises.prompt: Which summary best captures the shift from big CNNs to efficient CNNs?

options.text: Designers moved from relying mostly on brute-force scale toward more deliberate, budget-aware architecture choices
CNN research stopped caring about architecture and only cared about dataset names
Efficient CNNs removed convolutions from computer vision entirely
The only useful change was making filters visually prettier

exercises.explain: Right — the central change was learning to spend compute more intelligently.

exercises.prompt: True or false: SqueezeNet and MobileNet belong to the broader architectural shift toward making CNNs practical under tight hardware budgets.
exercises.explain: True — they are classic examples of efficiency becoming a design goal.

exercises.sentence: Efficient CNNs try to improve the tradeoff between accuracy and compute by making architecture choices more ___.
exercises.explain: Exactly — efficiency comes from intentional design, not luck.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best sets up the next architecture family, EfficientNet?

options.text: After learning that scale and efficiency both matter, the next step is asking how to scale depth, width, and resolution in a more balanced way
The next step is proving hardware constraints were never real
The next step is abandoning all model design and using only random filters

exercises.explain: Yes — EfficientNet continues the story by making scaling itself more systematic.

exercises.prompt: Looking across VGG, Inception, ResNet, DenseNet, SqueezeNet, and MobileNet, what is the strongest bridge into EfficientNet?

options.text: CNN design evolved from simply making networks larger toward finding smarter, more balanced ways to spend compute — so the next question is how to scale models systematically
The main lesson is that only the deepest possible model ever matters
The main lesson is that efficient models must ignore accuracy entirely
The main lesson is that architecture stopped changing after MobileNet

exercises.explain: Exactly. This unit showed that great CNNs are not just big — they are well-designed for their compute budget. Next up: EfficientNet, which asks how to scale depth, width, and image resolution together instead of pushing only one dimension at a time.
